Tool Call: retrieve("constitutional growth delay diagnostic criteria including bone age, puberty markers, family history")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

First state whether you agree/disagree with prior reasoning, give one new reason supported by evidence, then box your final answer.
\boxed{}
Based on the retrieved evidence, I agree with the prior reasoning that constitutional growth delay (CGD) is the most likely diagnosis. The patient has a delayed bone age (11 years vs. 14 years of chronologic age), absent pubic and axillary hair despite Tanner Stage 2 breast development—which is characteristic of CGD—alongside a positive family history of short stature. Importantly, evidence supports that in CGD, bone age lags behind chronological age and puberty delays occur without pathophysiological cause, allowing for spontaneous catch-up growth. Only ~3% of short-statured adolescents have an endocrinopathy requiring active treatment, reinforcing that initial management should focus on reassurance and regular follow-up rather than invasive tests.
